code

AI Mission Execution: Automate Business Tasks from Your Terminal

Published March 21, 2026 · 7 min read

What's a Mission?

A mission is a structured task that breaks down business work into executable steps. Instead of hiring someone to "write marketing copy" or "review code," you describe what you want, and the AI executes it—with planning, verification, and fallback strategies built in.

Think of it as giving instructions to a freelancer, but instant, deterministic, and repeatable.

The Mission Loop

User Input: "Write 5 LinkedIn posts about AI agents"
         ↓
Plan Phase: Break into subtasks
         ↓
Execute Phase: AI executes each subtask
         ↓
Verify Phase: Quality check and iteration
         ↓
Output: Finalized LinkedIn posts ready to publish

Key difference: Traditional APIs just return text. Missions return verified, polished results.

How Mission Execution Works

Step 1: Define the Mission

You provide a goal and context. The system breaks it into a plan.

mekong cook "Write 5 LinkedIn posts about AI agents"

Step 2: Plan (Optional, but Recommended)

The system generates a plan before execution. You review and approve.

# Proposed Plan
## Task 1: Research AI agent trends
- Search for latest AI agent frameworks
- Identify 3 key themes

## Task 2: Write 5 posts
- Post 1: What are AI agents? (Introduction)
- Post 2: Why agents beat traditional APIs
- Post 3: Building agents with LangChain
- Post 4: Real-world agent use cases
- Post 5: The future of autonomous AI

Approve? [y/n]

Step 3: Execute

AI executes each task in sequence, with progress updates.

🚀 Executing: Write 5 LinkedIn posts about AI agents

[1/5] Writing: "What are AI agents?" ...
✓ Generated 280 chars, optimized for engagement
[2/5] Writing: "Why agents beat APIs?" ...
✓ Generated 315 chars with comparison metrics

Use Cases: What Missions Solve

1. Marketing & Content

  • Blog posts: "Write 3 SEO-optimized posts on SaaS pricing" → 3,000+ word articles with headings, CTAs, internal links
  • Email sequences: "Create a 5-email onboarding drip campaign" → copy, subject lines, segmentation logic
  • Social content: "Generate weekly LinkedIn content calendar" → posts, threads, engagement hooks
  • Landing page copy: "Write headline + 3 value props for a CLI tool" → A/B test variants included

2. Engineering & Code

  • Code review: "Review this TypeScript PR for security and performance" → detailed feedback, suggested fixes
  • Bug analysis: "Debug this API timeout issue" → root cause, reproduction steps, fix
  • Architecture: "Design a credit-based billing system" → schema, API endpoints, edge cases
  • Documentation: "Generate API docs from this codebase" → OpenAPI spec, code examples

3. Business & Strategy

  • Business plans: "Write a 1-year business plan for a CLI tool startup" → market analysis, revenue model, milestones
  • Competitive analysis: "Compare 5 AI CLI tools" → feature matrix, pricing, positioning
  • Fundraising docs: "Create investor pitch deck outline" → problem, solution, market, traction, ask
  • Pricing strategy: "Design a fair billing model" → tiers, features, willingness-to-pay

4. Research & Analysis

  • Market research: "Analyze trends in generative AI" → key findings, data sources, implications
  • User research synthesis: "Summarize 20 customer interviews" → themes, quotes, actionable insights
  • Technical specs: "Write system design doc for event streaming" → architecture, trade-offs, implementation plan

Why Missions > Direct API Calls

Feature Direct API Missions
Planning Nope—just raw response Yes—breaks task into steps
Verification You review manually Built-in quality checks
Iteration Retry manually Auto-iterate on failure
Cost Per token Per completed mission (1-5 MCU)
Result quality Variable—depends on prompt Consistent—verification enforces standards

Example: Writing a blog post via API might take 3 retries because the first two are too short. With missions, the system auto-retries and verifies length/quality before returning.

Pricing: How Missions Use MCU Credits

Task Type Complexity MCU Cost
Blog post (500 words) Low (research + write + verify) 2-3 MCU
Email sequence (5 emails) Medium (copy + personalization) 3-4 MCU
Code review Medium (analysis + feedback) 2-3 MCU
Business plan (1 year) High (research + planning + writing) 8-10 MCU
Competitive analysis (5 tools) High (research + matrix + insights) 5-7 MCU

Pro tip: Use the --estimate flag to see MCU cost before executing.

Getting Started

Missions are available in OpenClaw (our CLI framework). Try your first mission:

# Install
npm install -g openclaw

# Sign up for free
openclaw auth:signup

# Run a mission (10 free credits to start)
openclaw cook "Write a marketing email for a new SaaS product"

Try AI Missions Free

Automate your business tasks. 10 free MCU credits, no card required.

Run Your First Mission
